questions.text: I notice that emotional exhaustion feels identical to physical exhaustion in my body.
I believe that being the person who cleans up after everyone else is a form of quiet leadership.
I am comfortable telling someone their approach needs to change.
I find that my emotional sensitivity means I need more downtime than most people.
I feel compelled to step in and correct others when they are off track.
I tend to carry the heaviest bag, take the longest walk, or accept the least comfortable seat without being asked.
I sometimes wish I could turn down the volume on my emotional responses.
I make many of my decisions based on what feels right rather than on careful analysis.
I feel that the moral priority of suffering is the conviction that has most consistently guided my choices over the course of my life.
I believe my personal dreams matter less than the collective good.
I happily take whatever position best supports the group's needs.
I can receive bad news without my body going into alarm mode.
I generally negotiate the terms of a collaboration with all parties.
I pick up on when a person positions a joke as if it just occurred to them even though it was planned.
I sequence my tasks so that each one builds logically on the one before.
I feel most at ease when I have minimal systems and maximum freedom.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the fact of human experience that no amount of progress or prosperity should allow us to forget.
I think that engaging with painful realities through structured reasoning is the method that is most respectful of the complexity and seriousness of what people are going through.
I believe that my emotional system has a built-in governor that keeps responses moderate.
I routinely calculate the return on investment for my personal time and money.
I believe that personal autonomy and self-sufficiency are among my highest values.
I notice when a speaker's gaze lingers on someone who challenged them earlier, even during an unrelated point.
I find that I do my best work when I am focused on my own advancement.
I use project management frameworks to coordinate complex work.
I prefer to frame requests as invitations rather than directives.
I rarely detect the difference between someone making small talk and someone trying to connect.
I am attuned to the split-second micro-expressions that reveal what someone actually thinks.
I rarely register the emotional atmosphere of a gathering.
I hold the view that the suffering of others should occupy a central, not peripheral, place in my moral reasoning.
I would rather lead a small group than follow in a large one.
I rarely notice when someone structures a question to guide me toward a particular answer.
I believe that success comes from staying open and responsive to emerging possibilities.
I think that the discipline of treating distressing situations as problems to be carefully reasoned through is the habit that most reliably leads to wise action.
I keep a record of decisions and the reasoning behind them for future reference.
My emotional reactions tend to be brief — they register and then dissipate naturally.
I believe that treating emotionally charged situations with deliberate calm is more productive than matching the emotional intensity of those involved.
